content.title: Bilinear Form
content.content:
A **bilinear form** on a vector space $V$ 
over $\FF$ is a function $B: V \times V \to \FF$ satisfying:

1. $B(\alpha u + \beta v, w) = \alpha B(u, w) + \beta B(v, w)$ (linear in first argument)

2. $B(u, \alpha v + \beta w) = \alpha B(u, v) + \beta B(u, w)$ (linear in second argument)
for all $u, v, w \in V$ and $\alpha, \beta \in \FF$.

content.title: Symmetric and Alternating
content.content:
A bilinear form $B$ is:

- **Symmetric** if $B(u, v) = B(v, u)$ for all $u, v$

- **Alternating** if $B(v, v) = 0$ for all $v$

- **Skew-symmetric** if $B(u, v) = -B(v, u)$ for all $u, v$

content.title: Matrix of a Bilinear Form
content.content:
Let $(e_1, \ldots, e_n)$ be a basis for $V$. Every bilinear form $B$ 
is represented by a matrix $A = (a_{ij})$ where $a_{ij} = B(e_i, e_j)$:
\[
B(u, v) = [u]^T A [v]
\]
where $[u], [v]$ are coordinate vectors.

$B$ is symmetric iff $A = A^T$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Change of Basis for Bilinear Forms
content.content:
If $P$ is the change of basis matrix, then the matrix of $B$ in 
the new basis is $P^T A P$.

Two matrices $A$ and $B$ represent the same bilinear form (in 
different bases) iff $B = P^T A P$ for some invertible $P$. 
Such matrices are called **congruent**.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Quadratic Form
content.content:
A **quadratic form** on $V$ is a function 
$Q: V \to \FF$ of the form $Q(v) = B(v, v)$ for some symmetric 
bilinear form $B$.

In coordinates: if $A$ is symmetric and $x = [v]$, then
\[
Q(v) = x^T A x = \sum_{i,j} a_{ij} x_i x_j
\]

content.title: Polarization Identity
content.content:
Over a field with $\text{char} \neq 2$, a symmetric bilinear form 
can be recovered from its quadratic form:
\[
B(u, v) = \frac{1}{2}[Q(u + v) - Q(u) - Q(v)]
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Quadratic Form on $\RR^2$
content.problem:
$Q(x, y) = ax^2 + 2bxy + cy^2$ corresponds to symmetric matrix 
$A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ b & c \end{pmatrix}$.

This is positive definite iff $a > 0$ and $\det A = ac - b^2 > 0$.

content.title: Signature
content.content:
The **signature** of a real quadratic form 
(or symmetric matrix) is the triple $(n_+, n_-, n_0)$ where:

- $n_+$ = number of positive eigenvalues

- $n_-$ = number of negative eigenvalues

- $n_0$ = number of zero eigenvalues (nullity)

content.title: Sylvester's Law of Inertia
content.content:
Two real symmetric matrices are congruent if and only if they have 
the same signature. Equivalently, every real symmetric matrix is 
congruent to a diagonal matrix of the form
\[
\diag(\underbrace{1, \ldots, 1}_{n_+}, \underbrace{-1, \ldots, -1}_{n_-}, \underbrace{0, \ldots, 0}_{n_0})
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Classification over $\RR$
content.content:
Real quadratic forms on $\RR^n$ are classified up to change of 
variables by their signature. There are $\binom{n+2}{2}$ equivalence classes.
content.label: Corollary

content.title: Definite Forms
content.content:
A real quadratic form $Q$ is:

- **Positive definite** if $Q(v) > 0$ for all $v \neq 0$

- **Negative definite** if $Q(v) < 0$ for all $v \neq 0$

- **Positive semidefinite** if $Q(v) \geq 0$ for all $v$

- **Indefinite** if $Q$ takes both positive and negative values

content.title: Characterization of Positive Definite
content.content:
For a real symmetric matrix $A$, the following are equivalent:

1. $A$ is positive definite

2. All eigenvalues of $A$ are positive

3. All leading principal minors are positive

4. $A = B^T B$ for some invertible matrix $B$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Non-degenerate
content.content:
A bilinear form $B$ is **non-degenerate** if
\[
B(u, v) = 0 \text{ for all } v \implies u = 0
\]
Equivalently, its matrix representation is invertible.

content.title: Orthogonal Complement for Bilinear Forms
content.content:
For a non-degenerate symmetric bilinear form and any subspace $W$:
\[
W^\perp = \{v \in V : B(v, w) = 0 \text{ for all } w \in W\}
\]
is a complement: $V = W \oplus W^\perp$ iff $B|_W$ is non-degenerate.
content.label: Theorem
